summary: This notebook demonstrates an inference pipeline for predicting chatbot preference rankings by adapting a previous LMSYS competition solution to the WSDM Multilingual Chatbot Arena competition. It loads a 4-bit quantized Gemma-2-9B model, merges two LoRA adapters using weighted averaging, and processes test prompts with custom tokenization that truncates inputs and balances response lengths. The pipeline optimizes inference speed through dynamic padding, length-based sorting, and parallel execution across two GPUs. Test-time augmentation is applied by swapping response roles and combining predictions with weighted averaging. Readers should learn how to efficiently deploy and ensemble LoRA-finetuned LLMs for sequence classification tasks under strict latency constraints.
competition_flow: Loads test data via PyArrow, tokenizes prompts and responses with custom truncation, merges two LoRA adapters into a Gemma-2-9B base model, runs parallel dual-GPU inference with test-time augmentation, and outputs a CSV submission with predicted winner probabilities.
data_reading: Reads test.parquet using PyArrow, converts to pandas DataFrame, and fills missing values with empty strings.
data_processing: Custom tokenization that prefixes prompts and responses with special tokens, truncates prompts to 512 tokens, splits remaining context equally between two responses, and adds BOS/EOS tokens. Applies test-time augmentation by swapping response_a and response_b.
ensembling: Merges two LoRA adapter weight files using a weighted average (0.7 and 0.3), then applies test-time augmentation by swapping response roles and averaging the original and augmented predictions with weights 0.45 and 0.55.
insights: Dynamic padding combined with length-based sorting significantly reduces inference latency.
Parallelizing inference across two GPUs using ThreadPoolExecutor maximizes hardware utilization.
Swapping response roles during test-time augmentation provides a simple but effective way to improve prediction robustness.
Weighted averaging of multiple LoRA adapters can effectively combine specialized fine-tuning directions.
